Project 01

Automated Digital Fitting & Human-Centric Simulation

PythonCLO3D APIUnity BlenderSystems Engineering Digital TwinVR
The Challenge

Developing a research prototype for a virtual fitting room to visualize the instantaneous "fit" of a garment on a personalized avatar based on real-world measurements. Collaborative project with a team of three.

Technical Solution
  • Designed an automation pipeline in Python utilizing the CLO3D API to orchestrate physical cloth simulations.
  • Developed a script to import morphological data (.avs), configure simulation iterations (utility_api.Simulate), and export the resulting 3D assets.
  • Integrated the custom-sized avatar and simulated garments into a Unity-based virtual environment for real-time VR immersion.
  • Note: While the internal CLO3D simulation is scripted, the asset bridge to Unity currently requires manual export-import steps, highlighting a key area for future workflow optimization.
Results

A functional end-to-end system linking morphological data to automated physical rendering, effectively reducing the reliance on physical prototypes.

Takeaway
  • This project demonstrates my ability to build repeatable workflows using code to transform complex input data into actionable design outputs and immersive experiences.
  • I demonstrated my creativity by designing an immersive VR interface and a Python Streamlit dashboard that makes digital simulations tangible for the end-user.
  • Working in a team of three, I focused on the technical hand-offs, proving my ability to coordinate tasks and integrate diverse software ecosystems like CLO3D and Unity.
CLO3D interface with Python API
Streamlit dashboard – user interface
Fitting room view in VR with Unity
Simulation of the fitting room to try a cloth in VR
Project 02

Motion Analysis Tool – Classification of Tennis Strokes

Machine LearningIMU Sensors QuaternionsPythonData Collection
The Challenge

Develop a tool to classify tennis stroke styles to help evaluate the impact of equipment parameters on an athlete's mechanical stress and performance.

Technical Solution
  • On-Court Data Acquisition: Led experimental testing with advanced competitive players, coordinating a synchronized network of wearable sensors (IMU) to capture high-frequency biomechanical data (acceleration and rotation/gyroscope data).
  • Advanced Signal Processing: Developed a robust pre-processing pipeline to manage multi-segment synchronization and resolved orientation ambiguities through hemispherical quaternion alignment.
  • Machine Learning Architecture: Engineered a systematic workflow from automated stroke segmentation to feature extraction, implementing a Random Forest model that achieved 84% accuracy in distinguishing technical styles (topspin vs. flat).
Results

An internal tool that links athlete biomechanics to product performance, facilitating injury prevention.

Scientific Status: Author of the research paper: "Inertial classification of topspin and flat forehand shots in tennis using wearable sensors," currently prepared for publication.

ML Pipeline
Forehand Stroke Classification (Topspin vs Flat) using IMU
Averages of Quaternion Components (w, x, y, z) — Topspin vs Flat
Takeaway
  • Leading this research in full autonomy allowed me to develop a proactive "Explorer" mindset, managing everything from high-frequency data collection to final ML validation.
  • My persistence was key in refining the signal processing algorithms and quaternions until reaching 84% accuracy, proving I don't give up when faced with noisy real-world data.
  • I learned to bridge technical gaps by translating complex results into clear insights for different audiences, from athletes on the court to engineers and managers.
  • I now know how to transform raw test data into engineering standards to effectively validate equipment performance.
On court data acquisition
On court data acquisition
Project 03

Industrial CAD Design & Prototyping — Cobot Interface

CAD (SolidEdge)FEA Analysis 3D PrintingErgonomics
The Challenge

Designing a custom mechanical interface to connect an induction brazing tool to a Fanuc Cobot to improve worker ergonomics.

Technical Solution
  • Conducted full 3D modeling under industrial constraints using SolidEdge.
  • Performed structural reliability validation via Finite Element Analysis (FEA) and thermal-mechanical simulations.
  • Adapted the design for 3D printing constraints to produce functional prototypes.
Takeaway
  • I demonstrated a strong "builder mindset" by iterating between SolidEdge designs and physical 3D-printed parts until reaching a fully functional prototype.
  • Collaborating with a senior industrial engineer allowed me to integrate rigorous factory safety and mechanical standards into my design process.
  • This work validated my ability to use FEA and thermal simulations to bridge the gap between digital modeling and real-world industrial reliability.
Technical drawing of the mounting plate
Photograph of the induction head housing
Cobot's fixation, pyrometer and lever part
Cobot interface
Printed parts
Project 04

Wind Turbine — Sustainable Design & Aerodynamic Modeling

SolidWorksSustainable Design Fluid DynamicsPhysics
The Challenge

Designing a high-performance wind turbine using recyclable materials, respecting a strict weight limit of <1 kg and specific box dimensions able to generate current to light a LED and charge a phone.

Technical Solution
  • Strategic Architecture Selection: Analyzed various wind turbine types and selected a dual-stage Savonius vertical-axis design for its superior low-wind start-up and omnidirectional capture.
  • Computational Optimization: Applied physics laws (Betz's limit, differential drag) to optimize the "S" shape of the blades for maximum torque.
  • Parametric Modeling: Used SolidWorks to create a lightweight, nested structure that fits precisely within the packaging constraints while maintaining structural integrity.
  • Material Science: Selected Spruce wood and flexible ABS for their balance of mechanical strength, vibration absorption, and recyclability.
Results

A fully functional prototype (base, mast, blades) capable of generating current to charge a mobile battery.

Takeaway
  • I practiced strategic decision-making by evaluating multiple architectures to identify the most efficient aerodynamic solution within strict constraints.
  • My positive mindset and team spirit helped align our technical choices with sustainability goals, ensuring a high-performance yet recyclable product.
  • This project confirmed my ability to solve real-world geometric problems through mathematical rigor and a systems-driven approach.
